coredata.dc:title: Multi-stimuli responsive hydrogels with shape memory and self-healing properties for information encryption
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Liuyan
Huang, Jingtao
Zhang, He
Yang, Tao
Mo, Zongwen
Qu, Jinqing
coredata.dc:description: With stimuli-responsive fluorescence and similar structure to extracellular matrix, smart fluorescent hydrogels are good candidate to encode data, especially in drug and food anti-counterfeiting. However, the development of these hydrogels in multistage information protection to achieve higher data security level is limited. Herein, multifunction smart fluorescent hydrogels were constructed and utilized to develop different multistage data encryption platforms. The hydrogels are a new type of fully physically cross-linked gelatin/metal complex associated poly(acrylamide-acrylate) double network gel. They displayed Fe3+ and H+-responsive fluorescence quenching, temperature or Fe3+-induced shape memory and self-healing properties. Combining the above abilities, several multistage data encryption platforms, from 2D to 3D data encryption, were designed. The results demonstrated a bright prospect for this smart fluorescent hydrogel in high security level anti-counterfeiting applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrogels
Information encryption
Multi-stimuli responsive
Shape memory
Self-healing

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence responsive studies of the hydrogels
Shape memory studies of the hydrogels

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration showing the preparation of the smart fluorescent hydrogels by UV polymerization and metal coordination, and demonstrating the various properties of the hydrogels, including self-healing ability, tunable fluorescence controlled by Fe3+ and H+, and shape memory modulated by temperature or Fe3+.

The Fe3+ and H+ responsive fluorescence quenching of the hydrogels. (a) Fluorescence titration of the hydrogel against the concentration of Fe3+. (b) Photos showing the fluorescence and color change of the hydrogels with different Fe3+ under daylight and UV light. (c) Emission spectra of the original hydrogels, the hydrogels with Fe3+, the Fe3+-containing hydrogels after reduction and the recovered hydrogels after oxidation. (d) Fluorescence changing cycles of the hydrogels (with 0.1 M Fe3+) by alternative titration of Vc/H2O2. (e) Emission spectra of the original hydrogels and the hydrogels with H+.

Shape-memory property of the hydrogels. (a) Photos showing the shape memory performance of the hydrogels induced by temperature. (b) Photos exhibiting the shape memory behavior of the hydrogels modulated by Fe3+ and recovered by reduction. (c) Shape fixity ratios of the hydrogels as the function of the heating time. (d) Shape fixity ratios of the hydrogels with different Fe3+ concentration. (e) Shape recovery ratios of the hydrogels versus the soak time in 0.1 M Vc aqueous solution.

The fabrication of 3D hydrogel geometries. (a) A five-petal flower-like hydrogel film. (b) The film was fixed to a bowl shape after self-healing and shape memory by the “heat & cool” process. (c) The combination of two hydrogel bowls formed a 3D hydrogel dodecahedron. (d) The hydrogel dodecahedron was recovered after another “heat & cool” process. (e) A 3D lantern-shaped hydrogel was prepared from a rectangular hydrogel film by the addition of Fe3+ and recovered after reduction.

2D multistage data encryption platforms based on the hydrogels. (a) Images shows that a word “SMILE” was printed on the hydrogel surface by Fe3+ and could be encoded and decoded by redox. (b) Illustration of the printing method of “NOT TRUE” and “88” characters by Fe3+ and H+. (c) Data protection by hiding correct data under misleading information. The characters “NOT TRUE” and “88” decrypted to be “TRUE” and “56” respectively after the reduction of Fe3+. Scale bars are 1 cm.

3D multistage data encryption platforms based on the hydrogels. The hydrogel film printed with (a) QR code or (b) numbers “123456” was folded and fixed to a crane shape or a cube by “heat & cool” process for information encryption. Another “heat & cool” process was required for successfully decryption. (c) The hydrogel strip printed with binary data “01110100” was twisted and fixed to a spiral shape by Fe3+ and formed 3D structure with fully concealed message. The decryption of the message was only available by reduction. Scale bars are 1 cm.

head.ce:title: Multi-stimuli responsive hydrogels with shape memory and self-healing properties for information encryption

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biotechnology and Health Sciences, Wuyi University, Jiangmen 529020, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Biotechnology and Health Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Biotechnology and Health Sciences, Wuyi University, Jiangmen 529020, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou 510640, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou 510640, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With stimuli-responsive fluorescence and similar structure to extracellular matrix, smart fluorescent hydrogels are good candidate to encode data, especially in drug and food anti-counterfeiting. However, the development of these hydrogels in multistage information protection to achieve higher data security level is limited. Herein, multifunction smart fluorescent hydrogels were constructed and utilized to develop different multistage data encryption platforms. The hydrogels are a new type of fully physically cross-linked gelatin/metal complex associated poly(acrylamide-acrylate) double network gel. They displayed Fe3+ and H+-responsive fluorescence quenching, temperature or Fe3+-induced shape memory and self-healing properties. Combining the above abilities, several multistage data encryption platforms, from 2D to 3D data encryption, were designed. The results demonstrated a bright prospect for this smart fluorescent hydrogel in high security level anti-counterfeiting applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Information encryption and protection are of great importance for governments, industries and society [1,2], especially for anti-counterfeit labels, which are widely used to fight for the trade of counterfeit goods, the second largest source of organized crime income [3]. Recent studies developed a variety of techniques and materials for information security applications, such as magnetic ink [4], laser holograms [5], plasmonic labels [6] and luminescence materials [7]. Among them, luminescent materials are attractive owing to their advantages of high throughput, convenient operation and facile design [8–10]. Generally, the information stored by these materials are only visible under external light irradiation. This, to some extent, increases data security, but their single and predictable readout process makes the recorded information still cloneable. Therefore, for safer data storage and protection, multiple security elements should be combined to obtain multistage data encryption. One favorite example is the fabrication of smart fluorescent materials which have stimuli-responsive fluorescent output [11,12]. Their chemical composition or structures is controlled by stimuli, leading to tunable fluorescent signals. Fluorescent materials that stimuli are invasive chemicals have been reported [13]. Information recorded in these materials is invisible under light irradiation until being triggered by specific chemicals. The chemicals are served as the keys and no one would know before being informed, revealing that these smart fluorescent materials are able to provide more effective data protection.

The above data encryption platforms are in 2D planes. Recently, some 3D platforms have also been developed, such as 3D polarization encryption [14–16]. This platform depends on the interaction between light polarization and matter, 3D structures were formed to encrypt information under a specific polarization state. Information cannot be found out without pre-knowledge of the polarization orientation. Another reported 3D data encryption platform was based on 3D surface-enhanced Raman scattering, a molecular-specific spectroscopic technique utilizing plasmonic nanostructures [17,18]. The specificity of Raman fingerprinting provides a distinction between spectroscopic features with resolution of 1 cm−1, which is beneficial to prevent data duplication. These 3D data encryption platforms further enrich the manners for multistage data protection, and offer less expectable decryption processes.

Although many smart fluorescent materials, such as nanoparticles [19], inorganic perovskite nanocrystals [20], carbon dots [21] and organic dye [22], have been studied for the applications of multistage data protection, smart fluorescent hydrogel are rare, especially in 3D data encryption [23]. However, these materials are promising because the intrinsic structure and characteristics of hydrogels closely resemble the native extracellular matrix [24,25], suggesting the potential application for these materials in anti-counterfeiting of pharmaceuticals and food, which is significant for human health.

Herein, we prepared a series of smart fluorescent hydrogels with multi-stimuli responsive fluorescence, shape memory and self-healing properties, and explored their ability in constructing different multistage data encryption platforms. As shown in Fig. 1 , the hydrogels were constituted by two physical cross-linked networks. The first network was a hydrogen bond-associated gelatin gel and the second network was a metal complex associated poly(acrylamide-acrylate) gel. Self-healing property of the hydrogels was discovered because of their non-covalent crosslinking nature. The hydrogels have strong fluorescence as the existing ligand in the second network, (E)-N-(4-(2-(2-hydroxybenzylidene)-hydrazinecarbonyl) phenyl) methacrylamide (HHPMA), formed metal complex with Al3+ [26]. Their fluorescence would be quenched by adding Fe3+ and H+, meaning the possibility of information storage on the hydrogel surface by using Fe3+ and H+ as “ink”. The hydrogels also have two modes of shape memory behavior, attributing to the temperature-controlled aggregation of gelatin [27] as well as the coordination between Fe3+ and the carboxylic acid groups [28] on the hydrogel skeletons. Considering the multiple function of this hydrogels and the easy redox of Fe3+ [29], four different multistage data encryption platforms were studied, including (1) data encryption and decryption by the redox of Fe3+ to deliver UV invisible information; (2) data encryption by delivering misleading information; (3) data encryption by hiding information in 3D hydrogel geometries; (4) data encryption by developing UV invisible information in 3D hydrogel geometries. The results displayed the remarkable potential of these smart fluorescent hydrogels in creating highly reliable and secure anti-counterfeiting products.

The arylhydrazone-based ligand, HHPMA, was synthesized according to our previous study [30] and has been proved to form blue fluorescent metal complex with Al3+. The smart fluorescent hydrogels were as-prepared by UV polymerization of acrylamide, acrylate and HHPMA as the first network and aggregation of gelatin as the second network. After the addition of Al3+, the targeted hydrogels were gained. The gelatin second network was significantly affected the mechanical properties of these hydrogels (Fig. S1), with ~40 folds increase of the fracture stress compared to the one without gelatin [30]. And we found that the hydrogel with 150 mg gelatin had similar fracture stress as the one with 200 mg, but exhibited higher stretchability. For this reason, this hydrogel was used as the representative for the following studies unless otherwise mentioned. Due to the fluorescent HHPMA-Al3+ complex, the obtained hydrogels displayed bright fluorescence and their fluorescence intensity decreased with the increase concentration of Fe3+ (Fig. 2 a). During the fluorescence quenching, their optical colors also gradually changed from pare yellow to dark red, but this color change was not obvious until the concentration of Fe3+ reached 0.03 M (Fig. 2b). It is notice that the fluorescence of the hydrogels was recovered by the addition of sodium ascorbate (Vc) as Fe3+ was reduced to Fe2+, while it was quenched again by adding H2O2 to oxidize Fe2+ back to its oxidized state (Fig. 2c). This fluorescence recovery and quenching cycle was repeatable with completely recovery after one cycle and partially recovery after two cycles (Fig. 2d). The fluorescence turn “OFF” of the hydrogels also occurred by adding H+ (Fig. 2e). However, the decrease of intensity was irreversible, probably because the imine group of HHPMA was destroyed under acidic condition [31], and further led to the disruption of HHPMA-Al3+ complex. Using Fe3+ and H+ as the “ink”, we successfully printed the fluorescent patterns to the hydrogel surfaces (Fig. S2, S3). As the fluorescence of the printed part was quenched, showing a sharp contrast towards the surrounding area, these patterns were clear enough to read out. Moreover, the pattern printed by Fe3+ was erasable using Vc solution and new pattern can further be printed by Fe3+, revealing a rewritable information input (Fig. S3).

Shape memory ability of the hydrogels was then investigated. Two modes of shape memory behaviors were found out. One was modulated by a “heat & cool” process: deforming the shape of a hydrogel film sample, heating it to 60 °C, and then cooling it down to room temperature to memorized the temporary shape (Fig. 3 a). This shape memory behavior was attributed to the gelatin network. It is known that an aqueous solution with 0.5–50 wt% gelatin above 40 °C is in sol state, and transforms to gel state after cooling due to the aggregation of gelatin polypeptides [32]. This characteristic of gelatin significantly influenced the mechanical properties of the hydrogels. The loss factor (tan δ) of the hydrogels increase with increasing temperature till 60 °C, indicating the elasticity decrease of the hydrogels upon heating (Fig. S4). As higher thermal stability also reported on the gelatin gel containing high concentration of metal ions [33], we set the heating temperature as 60 °C to analyze the temperature-controlled shape memory capability of our hydrogels. As shown in Fig. 3c, the shape fixity ratios of the hydrogels increased from 28.7 ± 2.8% to 94.4 ± 3.1% with the increase of heating time from 1 to 6 min. Only 6 min was required to get a shape fixity ratio larger than 90% indicated the shape memory of the hydrogels induced by temperature was fast and good. The shape memory behavior of the hydrogels was also modulated by adding Fe3+ to fix temporary shape and recovering shape by reduction (Fig. 3b). This shape memory behavior was owing to the metal coordination between Fe3+ and the carboxyl groups on the hydrogel skeleton. The coordination enhanced the crosslinking density of the hydrogels and further improved their mechanical strength (Fig. S5), so their shapes could be memorized by adding Fe3+. Moreover, as the concentration of Fe3+ increased, the shape fixity ratios of the hydrogels also increased and reached 87.8 ± 5.1% by using 0.3 M Fe3+ aqueous solution (Fig. 3d). The above hydrogel samples recovered quickly after being immersed in 0.1 M Vc aqueous solution for 20 s (Fig. 3e) owing to the reduction of Fe3+ to Fe2+ [34]. Taking the shape memory abilities and the Fe3+ and H+-responsive fluorescence quenching of the hydrogels into account, interesting free-standing decorations was created with some details only surprisingly showed under UV light (Fig. S6).

Because of the fully physical crosslinking nature of our prepared hydrogels, we assumed the hydrogels have self-healing property. Considering the gel-to-sol transition of gelatin network upon heating, we studied our assumption by incubating two cutting pieces of the hydrogel samples at 60 °C. The self-healing efficiency of these samples increase with increasing heating time. After incubating at 60 °C for 1 h and cooling down, the two pieces of the hydrogel sample rejoined to be a whole and was stretchable to about 650% of the original length (Fig. S7). Combining this self-healing property and the shape memory abilities of the hydrogels, we developed some 3D hydrogel geometries. A flat hydrogel film was cut into a five-petal flower shape (Fig. 4 a), deformed into a bowl shape, and then went through the “heat & cool” process. During the heating time, the hydrogel film also healed itself and finally formed a 3D bowl configuration (Fig. 4b). While rejoining two 3D hydrogel bowl together, a more complex 3D structure, 3D hydrogel dodecahedron was successfully fabricated (Fig. 4c). This hydrogel dodecahedron was able to recover into two pieces of flat hydrogel films upon heating and fixed this shape after being cooled (Fig. 4d). Except for temperature, it is also possible to construct 3D hydrogel geometries using Fe3+ solution. As shown is Fig. 4e, a 3D lantern-shaped hydrogel was prepared after treating a flat hydrogel film sample with Fe3+ solution, and as expected, it could be recovered to original 2D film shape after reduction.

Multistage data encryption and decryption approaches are crucial for confidential information protection. With tunable fluorescence quenching, shape memory and self-healing properties, our multifunction smart fluorescent hydrogels are ideal materials for these high-level security protection methods. Indeed, four different multistage data encryption platforms have been developed based on our hydrogels. For the first one, information “SMILE” was printed onto the hydrogel film as an example (Fig. 5 a). Relatively low concentration of Fe3+ solution (0.01 M) was selected to ensure the information was not visible under daylight, but at the same time, significantly quenched the fluorescence to obtain high-contrast patterns on the hydrogel surface. The information “SMILE” was completely masked after being immersed in Vc solution owing to the reduction of Fe3+. In this case, the information became invisible both under daylight and UV light. Oxidant (H2O2) was the only “key” to decrypt this information, illuminating an effective 2D data protection. We also studied another 2D platform, using both Fe3+ and H+ solutions for printing. Misleading information “NOT TRUE” or “88” was printed onto hydrogel film samples, and they all partly printed by Fe3+ or H+ solutions (Fig. 5b). As the fluorescence quenched by H+ was irreversible, the real information “TRUE” or “56” which printed by acidic solution was kept after reduction (Fig. 5c). Consequently, although the information encoded via this platform was able to read out simply under UV light and easy to be faked, the directly received information was incorrect and the real information was safely hided inside, effectively preventing data disclosure.

The third platform we designed was a 3D data encryption platform related to the fabrication of 3D hydrogel geometries. As an example, a square hydrogel film printed with a quick response (QR) code by Fe3+ solution was folded into a 3D crane shape by hand, and went through the “heat & cool” process (Fig. 6 a). Meanwhile, the 3D crane-shaped hydrogel structure was fixed by the reconfiguration of gelatin network caused by temperature variation. The QR code was securely encrypted inside the 3D structure and impossible to observe under neither daylight nor UV light. To decode the information, an additional “heat & cool” process should be carried out to recover the 3D structure upon heating and fix the recovered structure after cooling. Similarly, a 3D hydrogel cube was also prepared with message “123456” encoded inside (Fig. 6b). The self-healing properties of the hydrogels make it hard to finger out the original edges of the hydrogel sample before deformation. Therefore, the decryption of this cube must follow the above specific designed procedures. If it was unfolded by other way, such as external force, only wrong message would be obtained. Furthermore, another 3D data encryption platform was also developed and exhibited a stronger information hiding capability. As shown in Fig. 6c, binary data “01110100” that represented letter “T” was printed onto a hydrogel strip by acidic solution. The printed areas suffered from irreversible fluorescence quenching and clearly recorded the data. The hydrogel strip was then deformed to a 3D spiral shape by hand and immersed into 0.3 M Fe3+ solution for shape memory. During this period, the fluorescence of the hydrogel completely turned “OFF”. As a result, all the recorded information on the hydrogel spiral was entirely concealed and not possible to achieve a tiny bit even dismantling the 3D structure by external force or heating. Only the person who knows the decryption “password” – reductant (Vc) could successfully read the recorded data, making the data difficult to clone. Notably, the four different multistage data encryption platforms generated from our smart fluorescent hydrogels all shows high data security level and are promising in confidential information protection and anti-counterfeiting areas.

In conclusion, we have prepared a type of smart fluorescent hydrogels with tunable fluorescence, shape memory and self-healing properties, and developed four different multistage data encryption platforms based on these materials. Triggered by Fe3+ and H+, the fluorescence of the hydrogels turned “OFF”, and the one triggered by Fe3+ was reversible by the reduction of ferric iron to ferrous irons. Both temperature and the valency of iron ions were able to induce the shape memory capability of the hydrogels, and high shape fixity ratios (around 90%) were reached after heating the hydrogels at 60 °C for 6 min or adding 0.3 M Fe3+ solution. With the cooperation of the self-healing ability of the hydrogels and the above two functions, the hydrogels displayed excellent performance in the development of multistage data encryption platforms. Information that was invisible under both daylight and UV light was successfully encoded and decoded onto these hydrogels by the redox of Fe3+. It was also possible to cover the real information inside the misleading one on the hydrogel surface using both Fe3+ and H+ solution as the “ink” and decode the real information by reduction. 3D hydrogel geometries were further fabricated by a “heat & cool” process using the hydrogels printed with message and safely hided the message inside. We also designed 3D hydrogel geometries with H+ printed information to develop a UV invisible 3D data encryption platform using Fe3+ solution for shape memory. All the platforms studied in this work revealed the benefit of our multifunction smart fluorescent hydrogels in high security level data protection, opening up a promising future for these hydrogels in anti-counterfeiting industries.

Acrylic acid (99%) was received from TCI. Irgacure 2959 (≥98%) was obtained from Aladdin. Tween 80 (AR) and aluminum trichloride (≥99%) were purchased from Greagent, Acrylamide (99%), iron(III) chloride (99%) and sodium ascorbate (99%) were bought from Adamas. All the materials and solvents were used as received. HHPMA was synthesized according to our previous study [30].

3.5 mg HHPMA (2 wt% of AAm) and 178 mg AAm (2.5 mmol) were dissolved in 975 μL 10% Tween 80 aqueous solution. The mixture was vigorously stirred for 50 min to get a uniform emulsion. 25 μL AAc (15 wt% of AAm), 4.5 mg UV initiator, Irgacure 2959 (0.02 mmol) and 150 mg gelatin were added, and the whole system was charged by nitrogen. The precursor solution was heated up to 60 °C under stirring until gelatin completely dissolved. The solution was then injected into a mold consisting of two glass plates separated with a 1 mm thick silicone gasket and placed under UV light (365 nm wavelength, 10 W power) for 5 h to form an as-prepared hydrogel. The as-prepared hydrogel was immersed into 200 μL 1 M AlCl3 aqueous solution and the targeted hydrogel was formed after it completely absorbed the salt solution. Unless otherwise noted, the hydrogels using the above formula were used for all the measurements and experiments. Hydrogels with 100 mg and 200 mg gelatin were also prepared using the above method.

The hydrogel samples for tensile tests were cut into a dumbbell shape by a gel cutter with a width of 4 mm, a gauge length of 12 mm and a thickness of 1 mm. All samples were measured after being prepared for 24 h. The tests were performed using an Instron 3365 machine at 24 °C with a stretch rate of 100 mm/min. While testing, a thin layer of silicone oil was coated onto each sample to minimize water evaporation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence responsive studies of the hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: The hydrogel samples for investigation were rectangular with a dimension of 25 mm × 15 mm × 1 mm. These samples were immersed in various concentrations (0 – 0.1 M) of Fe3+ aqueous solution or 2 M HCl aqueous solution for 3 s, and then placed in the air for 15 min before study. For reduction or oxidation, the Fe3+-containing hydrogel samples were immersed in 0.1 M Vc aqueous solution or H2O2 aqueous solution for 3 s and tested after 15 min. The fluorescence properties of these hydrogel samples were measured by a Hitachi F-7000 spectrofluorometer at 25 °C. Emission spectra were collected at 346 nm excitation wavelength. The samples were put onto glass slides, then placed vertically into the spectrofluorometer, and at 45° facing the light source and detector to avoid the reflection of the source light entering the detector.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Shape memory studies of the hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: The hydrogel samples in these studies were cut into straight strips with a size of 25 mm × 5 mm × 1 mm. The samples were deformed to “U” shape by hand. For shape memory, they were either went through a “heat & cool” process, or immersed in different concentration (0–0.3 M) of Fe3+ aqueous solution for 5 s. During the “heat & cool” process, the deformed samples were incubated in a wet-chamber at 60 °C for 0–6 min, and then cool down to room temperature (around 25 °C). The reduction solution for shape recovery tests was 0.1 M Vc aqueous solution, and the hydrogel samples were immersed in this solution for 0–20 s. The shape fixity ratio was calculated as the ratio of the given angle over the fixed angle, while the shape recovery ratio was defined as the ratio of the recovered angle over its initial angle before shape deformation.

The hydrogel samples were measured by an Anton Paar model MCR-301 rheometer using a parallel plate of 25 mm diameter. The storage modulus (G′) and the loss modulus (G″) of the samples were swept at a constant strain of 1% and a frequency of 6% under a variety of temperatures from 15 to 75 °C. Silicone oil was coated around each sample to minimize water evaporation before test.

Filter papers were cut into different shapes and served as stamps for printing. The paper stamps were socked with 0.01 M Fe3+ or 2 M H+ solution and dried for about 5 min before use to get a precise pattern. For information printing, the paper stamps were placed onto the hydrogel surfaces for 2 min and removed after that. During this time, they were covered by a plastic film to avoid water evaporation. The Fe3+-printed information was eliminated after the reduction of Fe3+ at 0.1 M Vc solution.

Dumbbell shaped hydrogel samples were prepared with a same size as the samples for tensile tests. These samples were cut into halves and placed together again. After they were incubated in a wet-chamber at 60 °C for 10–60 min and cooled down to room temperature, these samples were used for tensile tests at a speed of 100 mm/min. The stretchability of the samples healed at 60 °C for 1 h was recorded by optical photos.

There are two ways to fabricate. (1) The hydrogel film samples were firstly heated to 60 °C, then folded to the desired geometries by external force, and temporarily held these shapes with the help of tinfoil. After they were incubated in a wet-chamber at 60 °C for 1 h and cooled down to room temperature, the 3D hydrogel geometries were obtained. (2) The hydrogel film samples were deformed to the temporary shape and immersed into 0.3 M Fe3+ aqueous solution for 3 s. After the residual solution on the hydrogel surface was dried in the air, the flat hydrogel samples became fixed 3D structures. For data encryption, information was printed onto the surface of the hydrogel samples before the above fabrication procedures.
The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2019A1515110679 and 2019A1515110226).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Data showing the mechanical properties of the hydrogels with different gelatin concentrations, the hydrogels after self-healing and the hydrogel with Fe3+; rheological study of the hydrogels as the function of temperature; photos of the fluorescent patterns printed by Fe3+ and H+; free-standing decorations created by the hydrogels.
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eurpolymj.2020.110061.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Physical unclonable functions generated through chemical methods for anti-counterfeiting

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Arppe, T.J. Sørensen, Physical Unclonable Functions Generated Through Chemical Methods for Anti-Counterfeiting, Nat. Rev. Chem. 1 (2017) 0031. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41570-017-0031.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Optical nanomaterials and enabling technologies for high-security-level anticounterfeiting

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Ren, G. Lin, C. Clarke, J. Zhou, D. Jin, Optical Nanomaterials and Enabling Technologies for High-Security-Level Anticounterfeiting, Adv. Mater. 32 (2020) 1–15. https://doi.org/10.1002/adma.201901430.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metal nanomaterials for optical anti-counterfeit labels

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.F. Smith, S.E. Skrabalak, Metal Nanomaterials for Optical Anti-Counterfeit Labels, J. Mater. Chem. C. 5 (2017) 3207–3215. https://doi.org/10.1039/c7tc00080d.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent and magnetic anti-counterfeiting realized by biocompatible multifunctional silicon nanoshuttle-based security ink

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Song, H. Wang, Y. Zhong, B. Chu, Y. Su, Y. He, Fluorescent and Magnetic Anti-Counterfeiting Realized by Biocompatible Multifunctional Silicon Nanoshuttle-Based Security Ink, Nanoscale. 10 (2018) 1617–1621. https://doi.org/10.1039/c7nr06337g.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A new anti-counterfeiting feature relying on invisible luminescent full color images printed with lanthanide-based inks

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Andres, R.D. Hersch, J.E. Moser, A.S. Chauvin, A New Anti-Counterfeiting Feature Relying on Invisible Luminescent Full Color Images Printed with Lanthanide-Based Inks, Adv. Funct. Mater. 24 (2014) 5029–5036. https://doi.org/10.1002/adfm.201400298.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Self-assembly of coordination polymers on plasmonic surfaces for computer vision decodable, unclonable and colorful security labels

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Han, Y. Liu, F. Li, Y. Lu, H. Cheng, Y. Lin, T. Zhao, S.H. Ng, U. Bach, Y. Zheng, Self-Assembly of Coordination Polymers on Plasmonic Surfaces for Computer Vision Decodable, Unclonable and Colorful Security Labels, J. Mater. Chem. C. 7 (2019) 13040–13046. https://doi.org/10.1039/c9tc04615a.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Upconversion Nanoparticles@Carbon Dots@Meso-SiO 2 sandwiched core-shell nanohybrids with tunable dual-mode luminescence for 3D anti-counterfeiting barcodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Tan, G. Gong, S. Xie, Y. Song, C. Zhang, N. Li, D. Zhang, L. Xu, J. Xu, J. Zheng, Upconversion Nanoparticles@Carbon Dots@Meso-SiO 2 Sandwiched Core–Shell Nanohybrids with Tunable Dual-Mode Luminescence for 3D Anti-Counterfeiting Barcodes, Langmuir. 35 (2019) 11503–11511. https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.langmuir.9b01919.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lanthanide-doped Core@Multishell nanoarchitectures: multimodal excitable upconverting/downshifting luminescence and high-level anti-counterfeiting

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Huang, J. Chen, Y. Liu, J. Lin, S. Wang, F. Huang, D. Chen, Lanthanide-Doped Core@Multishell Nanoarchitectures: Multimodal Excitable Upconverting/Downshifting Luminescence and High-Level Anti-Counterfeiting, Small. 16 (2020) 1–12. https://doi.org/10.1002/smll.202000708.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multicolor ultralong organic phosphorescence through alkyl engineering for 4D coding applications
